name: US Army Active Duty Strength
description: As of late 2025, the US Army maintains approximately 455,000-456,000 active duty soldiers, the largest branch of the US military. The Army has invested heavily in modernization and multi-domain operations, integrating AI, robotics, and long-range precision fires. In a potential Iran conflict, Army forces would provide theater missile defense (Patriot and THAAD batteries), ground-based logistics, and force protection for forward-deployed troops across CENTCOM AOR bases in Kuwait, Iraq, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, UAE, and Bahrain. Key constraints include the strain of sustained Middle East rotations since October 2023, recruitment challenges in prior years (though FY2025 met targets), and the political sensitivity of deploying large ground formations given the 21% pre-war public support for Iran strikes. The Army's 82nd Airborne and 101st Airborne remain rapid-deployment capable, but any large-scale ground commitment would require months of buildup and Congressional debate.
unit: active duty soldiers
political_cost: Large ground deployments face severe domestic opposition given sub-45% public support and no AUMF; rapid reaction forces (brigade-level) deployable without major political cost.
source: DMDC Active Duty Personnel Report (October 2025); USAFacts (December 2025); CRS IN12449 FY2025 NDAA End-Strength

name: Navy Carrier Strike Groups
description: The US Navy maintains 11 carrier strike groups, each centered on a nuclear-powered supercarrier with an embarked air wing of 65-70 aircraft, plus Aegis cruisers and destroyers. As of January 2026, USS Abraham Lincoln (CVN-72) with CSG-3 was deployed to the South China Sea and subsequently redirected to the Arabian Sea by late January. USS Gerald R. Ford (CVN-78) with CSG-12 was operating in the Caribbean/Atlantic area, available for rapid redeployment. USS George Washington (CVN-73) was forward-deployed in Yokosuka, Japan. USS George H.W. Bush (CVN-77) was in pre-deployment workups. The total battle force numbered approximately 293 ships. In an Iran conflict, CSGs would provide the primary strike platform — each carrier air wing can generate 150+ sorties per day with F/A-18E/F Super Hornets, EA-18G Growlers, and F-35C Lightning IIs. Escorts carry Tomahawk cruise missiles for land-attack and SM-2/SM-6 for air defense. Key limitation: redeploying carriers from Indo-Pacific creates vulnerability windows vis-a-vis China, as acknowledged by Japanese and Taiwanese officials.
unit: carrier strike groups (total); 2-3 deployable to CENTCOM within weeks
political_cost: Carrier deployments are executive-authority decisions requiring no Congressional approval, but Indo-Pacific allies express serious concern about redeployment away from China deterrence.
source: USNI News Fleet Tracker (January 12, 2026; December 29, 2025); Wikipedia Carrier Strike Group

name: B-2 Spirit Stealth Bomber Fleet
description: The US Air Force operates approximately 19-20 B-2A Spirit stealth bombers (509th Bomb Wing, Whiteman AFB, Missouri), the only platform capable of delivering the GBU-57A/B Massive Ordnance Penetrator — a 30,000-lb bunker-buster designed specifically for deeply buried hardened targets like Iran's Fordow enrichment facility. In the June 2025 Operation Midnight Hammer, 7 B-2s dropped 14 MOPs on Fordow, marking the first-ever combat use of the weapon. The B-2 can penetrate advanced air defenses undetected, carry up to two MOPs per sortie, and strike from CONUS with aerial refueling (round-trip missions of 30+ hours). Constraints include the small fleet size, intensive maintenance requirements (each B-2 requires specialized climate-controlled hangars), and limited MOP inventory — estimated at fewer than 30 weapons total. The fleet is aging and maintenance-intensive, with availability rates reportedly below 50% at times.
unit: aircraft
political_cost: B-2 strikes from CONUS are operationally invisible to the public until announced; politically low-cost compared to forward basing, but their use signals major escalation.
source: IISS Military Balance 2025; CRS Reports on B-2 fleet; Operation Midnight Hammer reporting (June 2025)

name: B-52H Stratofortress / B-1B Lancer Bomber Fleet
description: The USAF operates approximately 76 B-52H Stratofortress bombers (aging but continually upgraded, capable of carrying conventional cruise missiles including AGM-158 JASSM-ER, gravity bombs, and mines) and approximately 45 B-1B Lancers (supersonic conventional bomber capable of carrying the largest conventional payload in the USAF inventory — up to 75,000 lbs). Both types have extensive combat history in Middle East operations. In an Iran conflict, B-52s would serve as standoff cruise missile trucks, launching JASSMs from outside Iranian air defense range, while B-1Bs could deliver massive conventional payloads against area targets. B-52s can also deploy naval mines. The B-1B fleet has faced chronic readiness issues, with mission-capable rates historically around 40-50%. The B-52 fleet is undergoing re-engining (CERP program) but remains the most reliable heavy bomber. Both types require air superiority or standoff capability to operate against contested airspace.
unit: aircraft (76 B-52H + 45 B-1B)
political_cost: Conventional bomber sorties carry relatively low political cost; forward basing at Diego Garcia or Gulf airbases is routine and raises minimal domestic controversy.
source: IISS Military Balance 2025; USAF Almanac 2025; Air & Space Forces Magazine

name: THAAD Missile Defense Batteries
description: The US Army operates 8 Terminal High Altitude Area Defense (THAAD) batteries globally — the most capable theater ballistic missile defense system, designed to intercept short-, medium-, and intermediate-range ballistic missiles during their terminal phase. Each battery includes AN/TPY-2 radar (replacement cost ~$500 million per unit), 6 launchers, 48 interceptors, and fire control systems. As of January 2026, THAAD batteries were deployed to Israel, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Guam, and had one unit redeployed from South Korea. The June 2025 war consumed approximately 150 THAAD interceptors (~25% of total US THAAD stockpile ever funded), and Lockheed Martin produced only 96 THAAD interceptors in all of 2025. Each interceptor costs $12-15 million, creating a severe cost asymmetry against Iranian ballistic missiles. Prior Ukraine commitments had already strained the Patriot system ecosystem. The interceptor production bottleneck is the single most critical military constraint — stocks cannot be replenished at combat consumption rates.
unit: batteries (global total)
political_cost: THAAD redeployment from South Korea to the Middle East is politically sensitive with Seoul and creates perceived vulnerability on the Korean Peninsula; Japan and Taiwan have expressed serious concern.
source: IISS Military Balance 2025; Project knowledge verified status report (interceptor consumption and production data); US Military Times

name: Patriot Air Defense Systems (PAC-3 MSE)
description: The US Army fields approximately 15+ Patriot battalions with PAC-3 MSE interceptors, the backbone of US and allied theater air defense against tactical ballistic missiles, cruise missiles, and aircraft. Patriot batteries are deployed across the CENTCOM AOR — in Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Jordan, and Iraq — as well as in NATO Europe, South Korea, and Japan. Prior to January 2026, 10 Patriot systems and associated interceptors had already been transferred to Ukraine, significantly drawing down US and allied stocks. Lockheed Martin produced approximately 620 PAC-3 MSE missiles in 2025, with plans to triple output to 2,000/year — but this increase is years away from delivery. Each PAC-3 MSE costs $3.7-$4.1 million versus Iran's $35,000 Shahed-136 drones, creating an approximately 100:1 cost ratio. At wartime consumption rates demonstrated in June 2025, US Patriot stocks could face critical depletion within weeks of sustained combat. US monthly Patriot production (~60-65 missiles) cannot keep pace with combat use.
unit: PAC-3 MSE interceptors produced in FY2025 (total stockpile classified but significantly depleted)
political_cost: Diversion of Patriot interceptors from Ukraine creates friction with European allies and undermines Ukraine support; diversion from Indo-Pacific reduces allied confidence in US extended deterrence.
source: CRS reports on Patriot procurement; Lockheed Martin production figures; Project knowledge (Ukraine transfer data, cost ratios)

name: Tomahawk Cruise Missile Inventory
description: The US Navy and surface fleet maintain a large inventory of BGM-109 Tomahawk Land Attack Missiles (TLAMs) deployed across Arleigh Burke-class destroyers (up to 96 VLS cells each), Ticonderoga-class cruisers (122 VLS cells), and submarine platforms (Virginia-class SSNs can carry up to 40 Tomahawks; Ohio-class SSGNs carry 154 each). The exact current inventory is classified but estimated at 3,500-4,000+ missiles, though operational availability depends on what is loaded aboard deployed ships. Tomahawks were used extensively in Operation Midnight Hammer (June 2025). They provide standoff precision strike capability at ranges of 1,000+ miles. Block V Tomahawk variants include maritime strike capability. Key limitation: Tomahawk stocks used against Iran cannot be simultaneously provided to Ukraine (as noted by CSIS analyst Max Bergmann). Production rates are approximately 100-150 per year, insufficient to rapidly replace large-scale combat expenditure. Additionally, Tomahawk is subsonic and vulnerable to modern air defenses, though Iran's air defense capability was significantly degraded in June 2025.
unit: estimated total inventory (classified; figure is open-source estimate)
political_cost: Cruise missile strikes from naval platforms are the lowest political-cost offensive option — no pilot risk, no basing country permission required, and launched from international waters.
source: IISS Military Balance 2025; CRS Naval Cruise Missile reports; CSIS analysis

name: Marine Expeditionary Forces and Amphibious Ready Groups
description: The US Marine Corps maintains approximately 172,000 active duty Marines organized into three Marine Expeditionary Forces (I MEF at Camp Pendleton, II MEF at Camp Lejeune, III MEF in Okinawa). The 31st MEU (Marine Expeditionary Unit), embarked aboard USS Tripoli (LHA-7) based in Sasebo, Japan, comprises approximately 2,200 Marines with organic aviation (AV-8B Harriers or F-35B Lightning IIs, MV-22 Ospreys, attack helicopters) and ground combat elements. As of January 2026, Tripoli and the 31st MEU were in the Western Pacific. MEUs are designed for rapid crisis response, embassy reinforcement, and limited expeditionary operations. In an Iran conflict, Marines would provide embassy security reinforcement, potential seizure of offshore oil platforms or islands, mine countermeasure support, and noncombatant evacuation operations (NEO). Large-scale amphibious assault against the Iranian coast is theoretically possible but would require massive force buildup, is not currently planned per open-source reporting (CSIS analyst Seth Jones noted the US is 'not deploying a major ground force'), and would face extreme political opposition.
unit: active duty Marines (total); ~2,200 in 31st MEU on Tripoli
political_cost: Marine deployments for force protection and NEO carry low political cost; any ground invasion of Iranian territory would face overwhelming domestic and international opposition.
source: DMDC (October 2025); USNI Fleet Tracker (January 12, 2026); IISS Military Balance 2025

name: Air Force Fighter/Attack Wings (F-15E, F-16, F-22, F-35A)
description: The USAF fields approximately 318,000-319,000 active duty airmen and maintains significant fighter/attack aircraft inventory including approximately 218 F-15E Strike Eagles (primary deep-strike platform), 936 F-16C/D Fighting Falcons (multi-role), 183 F-22A Raptors (air superiority), and 360+ F-35A Lightning IIs (fifth-generation multi-role). The Air Force also fields approximately 8,600 Space Force personnel. In an Iran scenario, F-15Es operating from Gulf bases (Al Dhafra UAE, Al Udeid Qatar, Prince Sultan Saudi Arabia) would provide the bulk of manned strike sorties with precision-guided munitions. F-22s would establish air superiority, though Iran's air force is aging and near-irrelevant (F-4 Phantoms, F-14 Tomcats, MiG-29s). F-35As provide stealth penetration capability for suppression of enemy air defenses. Key constraint: Gulf basing countries may restrict offensive operations from their territory, as seen historically. Three F-15E jets were shot down by friendly Kuwaiti air defenses during the post-February conflict, highlighting coordination risks in a coalition air environment.
unit: fighter/attack aircraft (approximate active inventory across types)
political_cost: Air operations from forward bases require host-nation permission, which Gulf states may restrict to defensive-only use; air strikes carry lower political cost than ground forces but pilot losses would rapidly erode domestic support.
source: USAF Almanac 2025 (Air & Space Forces Magazine); IISS Military Balance 2025

name: Nuclear Deterrent Posture (Strategic Triad)
description: The United States maintains a nuclear triad consisting of approximately 400 deployed Minuteman III ICBMs (silo-based at F.E. Warren, Malmstrom, and Minot AFBs), 14 Ohio-class SSBNs (with approximately 8-10 on patrol at any time, each carrying 20 Trident II D5 SLBMs with multiple independently targetable warheads), and the B-2/B-52 bomber leg capable of delivering B61 gravity bombs and AGM-86B ALCMs. The total deployed strategic arsenal comprises approximately 1,550 warheads under New START treaty limits, with a larger reserve stockpile. The nuclear posture vis-a-vis Iran serves exclusively as a deterrent against Iranian nuclear breakout or use of weapons of mass destruction. The US has not made explicit nuclear threats against Iran, and nuclear use would be politically catastrophic given the non-nuclear nature of the conflict. However, the deterrent implicitly constrains Iranian escalation calculus. Iran's 440 kg of 60% enriched uranium represents a latent breakout capability (weeks to weapons-grade material if centrifuges available, months to a year for weaponization), and the US nuclear umbrella is a factor in allied (especially Gulf state and Israeli) calculations about Iranian nuclear risk tolerance.
unit: deployed strategic warheads (approximate, per New START)
political_cost: Nuclear use against Iran would be globally catastrophic politically and is effectively off the table for any conventional conflict scenario; the deterrent value exists precisely because it is never used.
source: DOD Nuclear Posture Review; Federation of American Scientists Nuclear Notebook; Arms Control Association New START fact sheet

name: US Cyber Command (USCYBERCOM)
description: US Cyber Command, headquartered at Fort Meade, Maryland, is a unified combatant command responsible for cyberspace operations. It comprises 133 Cyber Mission Force teams (approximately 6,200 personnel) organized into Cyber National Mission Force (defending the nation), Cyber Combat Mission Forces (supporting combatant commands), and Cyber Protection Forces. In a conflict with Iran, USCYBERCOM would conduct offensive cyber operations against Iranian military command and control, communications networks, air defense systems, and critical infrastructure. Publicly acknowledged capabilities include the ability to severely degrade internet connectivity (Iran's internet was reduced to 4% of normal in the opening strikes of the later conflict), disrupt IRGC command networks, and conduct information operations. Defensive cyber operations are equally critical — Iran's IRGC-backed groups (CyberAv3ngers, APT33, APT55) have pre-installed backdoors on US company networks and have historically targeted US industrial control systems at water treatment plants and power grids. Constraints include the difficulty of sustaining cyber effects (targets can be rebuilt or rerouted), the risk of unintended escalation, and the challenge of operating simultaneously against Iran, Russia, China, and criminal ransomware actors.
unit: Cyber Mission Force personnel (133 teams)
political_cost: Offensive cyber operations are largely invisible to the public and carry very low domestic political cost; international legal frameworks for cyber warfare remain underdeveloped, providing operational ambiguity.
source: DOD Cyber Strategy; Congressional testimony; USCYBERCOM public statements; Project knowledge (Iranian cyber threat actor details)

name: Forward-Deployed CENTCOM Forces and Basing
description: As of January 2026, the United States maintained an estimated 40,000-45,000 troops across the CENTCOM area of responsibility, with a massive buildup underway described as the largest since 2003. Key installations include Al Udeid Air Base (Qatar, CENTCOM forward HQ, hosts $1.1 billion AN/FPS-132 early-warning radar), Al Dhafra Air Base (UAE), Prince Sultan Air Base (Saudi Arabia), Ali Al Salem Air Base (Kuwait), Camp Arifjan (Kuwait, Army forward HQ), Muwaffaq Salti Air Base (Jordan), multiple facilities in Iraq (Erbil, Victory Base Complex), Naval Support Activity Bahrain (5th Fleet HQ), and Camp Lemonnier (Djibouti). The buildup included redeploying THAAD batteries, Patriot units, additional fighter squadrons, and naval assets into the region. Key vulnerability: all forward bases are within range of Iranian ballistic missiles (Emad/Ghadr at 1,700-2,000 km), cruise missiles, and proxy drone/rocket attacks from Iraqi militias. The June 2025 war demonstrated Iran's willingness and ability to strike US bases directly. Force protection consumes enormous resources and interceptor stocks.
unit: approximate troops in CENTCOM AOR (pre-February 2026 buildup level)
political_cost: Troop buildup in theater is an executive decision, but the 50,000+ final deployment level approaches thresholds that historically trigger greater Congressional scrutiny and public attention.
source: CENTCOM Commander Cooper (post-war figure of 50,000+); USNI Fleet Trackers; IISS Military Balance 2025

name: SM-3 and SM-6 Interceptor Stocks (Aegis BMD)
description: The US Navy's Aegis Ballistic Missile Defense system, deployed across Arleigh Burke-class destroyers and Ticonderoga-class cruisers, provides theater and regional ballistic missile defense using SM-3 (exo-atmospheric intercept, ~$10-15 million each) and SM-6 (endo-atmospheric/terminal phase, >$2 million each) interceptors. Aegis BMD ships are a critical component of the layered missile defense architecture protecting US forces and allies in the Gulf. The June 2025 war consumed approximately 80 SM-3 interceptors. SM-6 is dual-capable for both air defense and anti-ship missions. Production rates for both are limited and cannot keep pace with wartime consumption — the same production bottleneck affecting THAAD and Patriot. The SM-6 travels at approximately Mach 4, which creates an interception challenge against Iran's claimed Mach 13-15 Fattah-2 hypersonic glide vehicle (though Western analysts are skeptical of Iranian speed claims). Total SM-3/SM-6 inventories are classified but assessed as insufficient for a prolonged conflict at June 2025 consumption rates.
unit: classified inventory; 80 SM-3s consumed in June 2025 alone
political_cost: Interceptor depletion is invisible to the public but creates acute strategic risk — allied nations (Japan, South Korea, Taiwan) are directly affected by reduced availability for their defense.
source: Project knowledge (June 2025 consumption data); CRS Navy Aegis BMD reports; IISS Military Balance 2025

name: Submarine Force (Attack and Guided Missile Submarines)
description: The US Navy operates approximately 49-50 attack submarines (SSNs: 29 Los Angeles-class, 3 Seawolf-class, 21+ Virginia-class) plus 4 Ohio-class guided missile submarines (SSGNs, each carrying 154 Tomahawk cruise missiles). Submarines provide covert strike capability, intelligence collection, and sea control. In an Iran conflict, SSGNs deliver massive standoff strike capacity (a single SSGN can launch more Tomahawks than an entire destroyer flotilla), while SSNs would conduct intelligence surveillance and reconnaissance (ISR), protect carrier strike groups, and potentially conduct special operations forces insertion. The submarine force is the one US capability Iran has essentially no counter to — Iran's anti-submarine warfare capability is negligible. Constraints include the small fleet relative to global commitments (Indo-Pacific, Atlantic, Arctic), and the persistent submarine maintenance backlog that keeps approximately 30-40% of boats in maintenance at any given time. Virginia-class production is behind schedule.
unit: submarines (49 SSN + 4 SSGN)
political_cost: Submarine operations are entirely covert and carry zero domestic political cost until missiles are launched; their redeployment from other theaters creates no visible political footprint.
source: IISS Military Balance 2025; CRS Navy Submarine Force reports

name: UN Security Council Veto Power
description: The United States holds permanent membership and veto power on the UN Security Council, enabling it to block any binding UNSC resolution it opposes. This is a critical diplomatic tool in an Iran conflict — Russia and China would almost certainly seek UNSC resolutions condemning US military action, demanding ceasefires, or imposing consequences. The US veto ensures no binding international legal constraint can be imposed through the UN system. However, the veto is a defensive tool — it blocks adverse action but cannot generate positive authorization (any US attempt to get UNSC authorization for action against Iran would be vetoed by Russia and China). The veto also does not prevent General Assembly resolutions (non-binding but symbolically significant), International Court of Justice proceedings, or regional diplomatic condemnation. Extensive US veto use on Middle East issues (particularly regarding Israel) has accumulated significant diplomatic costs, with much of the Global South viewing US veto practice as undermining UNSC legitimacy. This constrains US ability to build broad international coalitions.
unit: permanent UNSC seat with veto
political_cost: Each veto deepens perceptions of US double standards at the UN, eroding soft power and complicating coalition-building with Global South nations; however, the alternative — allowing adverse resolutions — is politically unacceptable domestically.
source: UN Charter Article 27; US voting record at UNSC

name: US-Israel Security Relationship (No Formal Mutual Defense Treaty)
description: The US-Israel relationship is the closest US security partnership in the Middle East, but it is not governed by a formal mutual defense treaty (unlike NATO Article 5 obligations). The US provides approximately $3.8 billion/year in military aid under the 2016 Memorandum of Understanding (10-year, $38 billion package), plus emergency supplemental appropriations. There is no legal obligation requiring the US to enter combat on Israel's behalf — US participation in the June 2025 operation was a policy choice, not a treaty requirement. This distinction matters for the simulation: the US retains theoretical freedom of action, but domestic political dynamics (AIPAC's $127 million/cycle spending reaching 65% of Congress, evangelical Christian Zionist base estimated at 10+ million voters, bipartisan consensus on Israel support) create de facto policy constraints that function similarly to treaty obligations. Secretary Rubio collected over $1 million in Israel lobby contributions since 2010. The simulation should model this as 'political treaty' rather than legal treaty — binding in practice through domestic incentive structures rather than international law.
unit: million USD/year in baseline military aid
political_cost: Breaking with Israel on a major military operation would trigger massive domestic political backlash from both parties; supporting Israel unconditionally alienates progressive Democrats, Arab/Muslim Americans, and increasingly some 'America First' conservatives.
source: 2016 US-Israel MOU; CRS Report RL33222; FEC filings via Track AIPAC; Project knowledge (AIPAC spending data)

name: NATO Alliance Implications
description: The US leads the NATO alliance of 32 member states, bound by Article 5 collective defense. However, a US-initiated conflict with Iran does not trigger NATO obligations (Article 5 applies only to attacks on member territory). As of January 2026, NATO allies have shown no inclination to participate in offensive operations against Iran — the post-February pattern (NATO allies almost universally refusing to participate, Spain denying base access) reflects pre-existing allied reluctance. The US can use NATO bases (Incirlik in Turkey, Souda Bay in Crete, Rota/Morón in Spain, Ramstein/Spangdahlem in Germany, and Aviano/Sigonella in Italy) for staging and logistics, subject to host-nation approval — which is not guaranteed. Turkey, a NATO ally with its own complex relationship with Iran, would almost certainly deny basing or overflight. NATO's European members are also acutely concerned about diversion of US air defense assets (Patriot, THAAD) from European theater and Ukraine support. The conflict fundamentally tests whether the US can sustain simultaneous commitments to NATO, Indo-Pacific deterrence, and a Middle East war.
unit: NATO member states
political_cost: US action against Iran without broad allied support deepens transatlantic fissures already strained by Trump administration NATO skepticism; European allies particularly alarmed by diversion of air defense from Ukraine.
source: NATO Treaty; European Council and allied government statements; Project knowledge (allied non-participation data)

name: Abraham Accords Leverage
description: The Abraham Accords (2020) normalized relations between Israel and the UAE, Bahrain, Morocco, and Sudan, with Saudi Arabia widely seen as the ultimate prize. The accords created a framework for Arab-Israeli security cooperation against shared Iranian threats. As of January 2026, this framework provides diplomatic leverage: Gulf states that normalized with Israel have a vested interest in containing Iran's nuclear and missile programs. However, the accords are under severe strain. Gulf states were not consulted before the June 2025 Israeli strikes, their territory and infrastructure are being struck by Iranian retaliation, and they have explicitly stated 'this is not their war.' Three or more GCC states were reviewing overseas investment commitments worth $2+ trillion in US assets. Saudi normalization, which was reportedly close before October 2023, is now indefinitely delayed. The accords' value as leverage diminishes if Gulf states perceive the US as unable to protect them — the fundamental security guarantee underpinning normalization. UAE and Bahrain provided air defense support but no offensive participation.
unit: Abraham Accords signatory states (UAE, Bahrain, Morocco, Sudan)
political_cost: The accords are a signature Trump diplomatic achievement; their unraveling due to an Iran conflict would be a major political liability, but Gulf state frustration is directed at both the US and Iran.
source: Abraham Accords texts (2020); Project knowledge (Gulf state posture and investment review data)

name: Unilateral Sanctions Authority (IEEPA / Executive Orders)
description: The President possesses sweeping unilateral authority to impose economic sanctions under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA, 1977), which allows blocking of assets, prohibition of transactions, and designation of individuals and entities during declared national emergencies. Iran is already subject to the most comprehensive US sanctions regime short of a total embargo — covering oil exports, banking, metals, shipping, petrochemicals, and virtually the entire Iranian economy. The Treasury Department's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) administers these sanctions. Additional authority exists under the Countering America's Adversaries Through Sanctions Act (CAATSA) and various Iran-specific executive orders. Key constraint: maximum pressure sanctions have already been applied — there is limited additional economic leverage to impose on Iran beyond what already exists. Secondary sanctions on Chinese entities importing Iranian oil through shadow fleets represent the most significant untapped tool, but imposing them would escalate US-China tensions dramatically. The sanctions regime has also created the perverse effect of pushing Iran closer to Russia and China economically.
unit: comprehensive sanctions regime (broadest possible already in effect)
political_cost: Sanctions enjoy broad bipartisan Congressional support; escalating secondary sanctions against China would be domestically popular but risks major economic retaliation from Beijing.
source: IEEPA (50 USC §§1701-1707); Treasury OFAC Iran sanctions program; CRS reports on Iran sanctions

name: Dollar Dominance and SWIFT Access Control
description: The US dollar facilitates approximately 80-90% of global oil transactions and comprises approximately 57% of global foreign exchange reserves (down from 72% in 2001). The US exercises significant influence over the SWIFT international payment messaging system (headquartered in Belgium but subject to US leverage through dollar clearing requirements). Iran was disconnected from SWIFT in 2012 and 2018 as part of nuclear-related sanctions. This 'financial nuclear option' gives the US the ability to effectively exclude any entity or country from the global dollar-denominated financial system. However, this weapon has diminishing returns: its overuse has accelerated development of alternatives including China's CIPS (Cross-border Interbank Payment System), Russia's SPFS, and the mBridge digital currency platform (which processed $55.5 billion in transactions by November 2025). BRICS nations are actively building parallel financial infrastructure. The yuan's share of global reserves remains only ~2%, and no imminent dollar replacement exists, but the trajectory of gradual erosion is clear and is being accelerated by the conflict. Each application of financial weaponization creates incentives for workarounds.
unit: dollar share of global oil transactions: ~80-90%; global reserves: ~57%
political_cost: SWIFT exclusion and dollar weaponization enjoy domestic support but accelerate de-dollarization efforts by adversaries; overuse risks undermining the very financial architecture that gives the tool its power.
source: Project knowledge (economic assessment document); IMF COFER data; BIS statistics

name: Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR)
description: The US Strategic Petroleum Reserve held approximately 415 million barrels as of early 2026, stored in underground salt caverns along the Gulf Coast. The SPR was drawn down significantly during the 2022 energy crisis (from ~600 million barrels) and has been only partially refilled. The President has authority to order emergency SPR releases; the maximum drawdown rate is approximately 4.4 million barrels per day. In an Iran conflict involving Strait of Hormuz closure, the IEA coordinated the largest emergency reserve release in history (400 million barrels total, with the US contributing 172 million barrels). However, SPR capacity provides only limited buffer: 415 million barrels equals roughly 20 days of normal Hormuz flow or about 22 days of total US oil imports. SPR releases provide temporary price relief (prices briefly dipped to $87/bbl after the March release) but do not address the underlying supply disruption. The SPR has been a politically contested issue — Republicans criticized the Biden-era drawdown, and refilling at higher prices is expensive.
unit: million barrels (approximate pre-conflict level)
political_cost: SPR releases are politically popular in the short term (lower gas prices) but criticized by deficit hawks and energy hawks who argue the reserve should be saved for true emergencies; further drawdown reduces future cushion.
source: DOE SPR inventory reports; Project knowledge (IEA release data, 172M barrel US contribution)

name: Ability to Pressure Allied Economies
description: The US exercises significant economic leverage over allied and partner economies through its dominance of global financial markets, the dollar's reserve currency status, and bilateral trade relationships. This includes the ability to grant or withhold sanctions waivers (as demonstrated by the March 2026 Russian oil sanctions waiver), set secondary sanctions enforcement priorities, and use trade policy as diplomatic leverage. US Treasury bond markets are the backbone of global finance, with roughly $38.9 trillion in total debt and approximately $1 trillion in annual interest payments. Gulf states hold an estimated $2+ trillion in US-linked investments. Japan imports 95% of its oil from the Middle East, making it acutely dependent on US naval protection of shipping lanes. South Korea, India, and European allies are similarly leveraged through energy dependence and financial integration. Key constraint: this leverage is a wasting asset — Gulf states are actively diversifying toward multi-vector alignment (China, Russia, India, Pakistan). The conflict itself accelerates this diversification as allies question US reliability. Three or more GCC states were already reviewing their overseas investment commitments.
unit: qualitative — economic leverage over key allies and partners
political_cost: Coercive economic diplomacy with allies generates resentment and accelerates the very diversification it seeks to prevent; allies distinguish between being partners and being vassals.
source: Project knowledge (Gulf state investment review, Japan energy dependence, economic assessment); US Treasury data

name: Satellite Reconnaissance (NRO / Commercial)
description: The National Reconnaissance Office (NRO) operates the US government's fleet of classified reconnaissance satellites, generally understood to include electro-optical imaging satellites (KH-11 series, believed to achieve resolution of ~10 cm or better), synthetic aperture radar (SAR) satellites for all-weather imaging, signals intelligence (SIGINT) satellites, and missile early-warning satellites (Space-Based Infrared System — SBIRS). This constellation is supplemented by a rapidly growing commercial satellite imagery sector (Maxar, Planet Labs, BlackSky, Capella Space) providing persistent surveillance with revisit times measured in hours. For an Iran conflict, satellite reconnaissance provides: pre-strike targeting (identification of mobile missile launchers, air defense sites, leadership locations), battle damage assessment, monitoring of Iranian naval and mining activity in the Strait of Hormuz, tracking of enriched uranium movements (to the extent possible), and early warning of ballistic missile launches. The June 2025 strikes were enabled by satellite-derived targeting. Key limitation: Iran has adopted denial and deception measures including deeply buried facilities, mobile launchers, and tunnel networks. The IAEA stated in February 2026 it 'cannot provide any information on the current size, composition or whereabouts' of Iran's enriched uranium stockpile — highlighting the limits of even the best technical collection.
unit: classified constellation size; supplemented by commercial providers
political_cost: Satellite reconnaissance carries no domestic political cost; commercial imagery increasingly enables open-source verification of government claims, which can be politically inconvenient.
source: NRO public statements; Congressional testimony on satellite programs; IAEA February 2026 report (on intelligence limitations)

name: Intelligence Sharing with Israel (Special Relationship)
description: The US-Israel intelligence relationship is among the closest bilateral intelligence partnerships in the world, encompassing signals intelligence (NSA-Unit 8200 cooperation), human intelligence coordination, satellite imagery sharing, and real-time threat warning. Israel's Unit 8200 is one of the world's most capable SIGINT organizations and provides the US with unique collection against Middle Eastern targets. The CIA and Mossad coordinate closely on Iran — the combination of CIA human assets and Israeli penetration of Iranian networks provided the intelligence for both Operation Midnight Hammer (June 2025) and the later leadership targeting. Israeli military had access to 'nearly all' Tehran traffic cameras and, working with CIA, used this access for targeting the strikes that killed Khamenei. The relationship also creates constraints: Israel's willingness to share intelligence is contingent on US policy alignment, and Israeli operations sometimes create fait accompli situations for the US (as when Netanyahu called Trump on February 23 with Khamenei's location). The intelligence relationship effectively gives Israel significant influence over US threat assessments and targeting decisions regarding Iran.
unit: qualitative — deepest bilateral intelligence partnership in the region
political_cost: The intelligence relationship is invisible to the public and carries no direct political cost, but Israeli intelligence influence on US decision-making (as in the February 23 call) creates dependency that limits US independent judgment.
source: Project knowledge (CIA-Mossad cooperation details, traffic camera access, February 23 call); open-source reporting on Unit 8200-NSA cooperation

name: Intelligence Sharing with Gulf States (GCC Partners)
description: The US maintains intelligence-sharing arrangements with Gulf Cooperation Council states, particularly Saudi Arabia (General Intelligence Presidency), UAE (Signals Intelligence Agency), and Bahrain (which hosts the US 5th Fleet and provides local maritime intelligence). These partnerships provide human intelligence networks in the Gulf, regional signals intelligence, and critical basing and logistics support for US intelligence operations. Gulf states provide unique access to Iranian diaspora communities, commercial and financial intelligence on Iranian sanctions evasion, and cultural/linguistic expertise. The relationships are governed by bilateral intelligence-sharing agreements and are mediated through the CIA's Near East Division and DIA's CENTCOM intelligence directorate. Key constraint: Gulf states maintain their own backchannel relationships with Iran (particularly Oman and Qatar) and do not share all intelligence with the US. Trust in the US intelligence partnership has been severely eroded by the perception that the US failed to protect Gulf states from Iranian retaliation despite the massive US military presence. Three or more GCC states are reviewing their security relationships, and Gulf intelligence services may increasingly hedge by sharing with non-US partners.
unit: qualitative — multiple bilateral intelligence partnerships across GCC
political_cost: Gulf intelligence cooperation is invisible to US domestic politics, but Gulf state frustration with US decision-making (not being consulted before June 2025 strikes, territory being attacked) risks degradation of cooperative relationships.
source: IISS; Open-source reporting on US-Gulf intelligence cooperation; Project knowledge (Gulf state trust erosion, backchannel relationships)

name: NSA Signals Intelligence Collection
description: The National Security Agency is the world's premier signals intelligence organization, with capabilities encompassing interception of communications (COMINT), electronic signals (ELINT), and foreign instrumentation signals (FISINT). Against Iran, NSA collection targets include IRGC command and control communications, diplomatic traffic, missile telemetry, nuclear program-related communications, and cyberspace surveillance. The NSA operates collection facilities globally, including sites in the Gulf region, and taps undersea cables, satellite communications, and internet backbone infrastructure. NSA's capabilities were critical in monitoring Iranian missile preparations in both June 2025 and the subsequent period, providing early warning of launch sequences and enabling interceptor cueing. Key limitations: Iran has invested significantly in communications security, including indigenous encryption systems and operational security measures learned from previous compromises (e.g., the Stuxnet revelation). The IRGC increasingly uses courier-based and hardened communications for the most sensitive operations. Russia's confirmed provision of satellite imagery and targeting intelligence to Iran represents a counterintelligence challenge — Russia may also be sharing insights about US collection methods with Iranian intelligence services.
unit: qualitative — world's largest signals intelligence organization
political_cost: NSA operations carry no direct domestic political cost but are subject to FISA oversight requirements; revelations about collection scope (as in 2013 Snowden disclosures) can create political backlash.
source: Congressional testimony on intelligence capabilities; IISS; Open-source assessments of NSA capabilities

name: Munitions Production Capacity and Industrial Base
description: The US defense industrial base produces key munitions at rates that are insufficient for sustained high-intensity combat. Verified production rates as of 2025: Lockheed Martin produced 620 PAC-3 MSE missiles and 96 THAAD interceptors; Raytheon produces approximately 60-65 Patriot missiles per month; Tomahawk production is approximately 100-150 per year; JASSM/JASSM-ER production is similarly constrained. A deal to triple PAC-3 output to 2,000/year and quadruple THAAD to 400/year has been announced but is 'years away' from delivery. The June 2025 war consumed over $2 billion in THAAD interceptors alone, and replacing 800 interceptors fired would require 15+ months at 2025 production rates. This production bottleneck is arguably the most critical strategic constraint on US ability to sustain a prolonged conflict with Iran. The cost asymmetry is devastating: Iran spends $35,000 per Shahed-136 drone while the US spends $3.7-15 million per interceptor — a 100:1 to 350:1 ratio. Iran can produce 50-100+ missiles per month, aided by approximately 3,000 tons of sodium perchlorate received from China. The US industrial base was not designed for attrition warfare against a peer-level missile threat.
unit: production rates: 620 PAC-3/yr, 96 THAAD/yr, ~100-150 Tomahawk/yr
political_cost: Munitions shortages are not yet a major public issue but could become one if the war extends and allied (Ukraine, Indo-Pacific) stocks are visibly depleted; defense industry lobbying for increased procurement is bipartisan.
source: Lockheed Martin production reports; Project knowledge (consumption rates, cost ratios, Chinese sodium perchlorate supply); CRS munitions production reports

name: Congressional AUMF Requirements and War Powers
description: Under the War Powers Resolution of 1973, the President must notify Congress within 48 hours of committing US forces to hostilities and must withdraw forces within 60 days (with a 30-day extension) unless Congress authorizes continued operations. No Authorization for Use of Military Force (AUMF) has been passed or sought for Iran as of January 2026. The administration has relied on Article II commander-in-chief authority and existing AUMFs (2001 AUMF against al-Qaeda and associated forces, 2002 Iraq AUMF — though the 2002 AUMF was repealed in 2024). Congressional War Powers resolutions to constrain the Iran conflict were defeated narrowly: 47-53 in the Senate and 212-219 in the House, with only one Republican (Rand Paul) and one Democrat (Fetterman) crossing party lines. This narrow margin means the executive has political authorization to continue, but formal legal authorization is contested. The lack of AUMF creates legal vulnerability — service members' families could potentially challenge the legality of operations, and future administrations could cite the lack of authorization to reverse course. Congress historically takes 6-8 years to translate majority opposition into policy change (Vietnam, Iraq precedent).
unit: no AUMF; War Powers resolutions defeated by 6 (Senate) and 7 (House) votes
political_cost: Operating without AUMF is legally risky but politically manageable as long as GOP holds ranks; narrow defeat margins mean any casualty spike or scandal could flip enough votes to force constraints; 2026 midterms (November) loom as the decisive political checkpoint.
source: War Powers Resolution (50 USC §§1541-1548); Project knowledge (vote tallies, partisan breakdown, historical comparison)

name: US Defense Budget and Fiscal Capacity
description: The US defense budget for FY2025 was approximately $886 billion (constrained by the Fiscal Responsibility Act of 2023), with the FY2026 budget expected to exceed $895 billion. Total gross national debt stood at approximately $38.9 trillion as of early 2026, growing at $7.23 billion per day, with annual interest payments reaching approximately $970 billion in FY2025 and projected to exceed $1 trillion in FY2026 — now exceeding both national defense and Medicaid spending. The average interest rate on total marketable debt has risen to 3.355% (up 122% from five years earlier), and 33% of publicly held marketable debt matures within 12 months, meaning higher rates flow through quickly. Military operations in the Iran theater cost approximately $1.9 billion per day during active combat (extrapolated from $11.3 billion over six days). This fiscal context constrains sustained high-intensity operations: the US is simultaneously spending ~$50 billion per week on debt service and ~$13 billion per week on military operations. Treasury yields rose 29 basis points in the early weeks of the conflict. The Suez Crisis parallel — where military overextension at an energy chokepoint accelerated reserve currency erosion — is frequently cited by economists.
unit: billion USD (FY2025 defense budget); $38.9T total national debt
political_cost: Deficit spending for military operations faces growing bipartisan concern; interest payments exceeding defense spending creates a powerful fiscal constraint narrative; gas price increases ($3.60+) compound public economic anxiety heading into midterms.
source: CBO budget projections; FY2025 NDAA (P.L. 118-159); Project knowledge (debt, interest, operational cost data); Joint Economic Committee

name: Publicly Acknowledged Open-Source Intelligence Assessments on Iran
description: Multiple US intelligence community assessments on Iran's capabilities and intentions have been made public through Congressional testimony, leaks, and official statements as of January 2026. Key publicly known assessments include: DIA leaked assessment (June 24, 2025) that Operation Midnight Hammer set back Iran's nuclear program 'months' only with 'low confidence'; Pentagon assessment (July 2, 2025) of 'one to two years' setback; DNI Gabbard/CIA Ratcliffe revised assessment claiming 'years to rebuild'; IAEA Director Grossi (September 2025) confirming 'almost all sensitive equipment' at Fordow destroyed but Iran retains capability to rebuild 'in a matter of months'; White House internal document (November 2025, leaked) acknowledging strikes only 'significantly degraded' rather than 'obliterated' the program; and IAEA's February 2026 admission it 'cannot provide any information' on the current location of Iran's enriched uranium stockpile. These competing assessments — ranging from 'months' to 'years' to 'obliterated' — represent a significant intelligence gap and policy disagreement within the US government. The simulation should model this uncertainty rather than assuming any single assessment is correct.
unit: qualitative — multiple competing classified and declassified assessments
political_cost: Conflicting intelligence assessments undermine public trust in government claims (echoing WMD credibility gap from Iraq); NBC News tracked at least 8 different shifting official justifications for the conflict.
source: Project knowledge (full table of competing assessments); Congressional testimony; IAEA reports; leaked DIA and White House assessments

name: Mine Countermeasure Capability
description: The US Navy's mine countermeasure (MCM) capability has undergone significant transition. All four Avenger-class mine countermeasure ships previously based in Bahrain were decommissioned, replaced by Littoral Combat Ships USS Canberra (LCS-30), USS Tulsa (LCS-16), and USS Santa Barbara (LCS-32) operating with the Navy's first operational mine countermeasure mission packages in US 5th Fleet. These ships utilize remotely operated mine hunting systems, autonomous underwater vehicles, and airborne mine countermeasure helicopters (MH-53E Sea Dragon). Iran possesses an estimated 5,000-6,000 naval mines (contact, influence, and rising mines) and has begun mining the Strait of Hormuz. Mine-clearing in a contested strait takes months under the best circumstances, and Iran's mining campaign represents one of the most cost-effective asymmetric threats to US naval operations. The US MCM force is widely assessed as under-resourced for the scale of threat — three LCS-based MCM packages is a fraction of what would be needed to clear the Strait. Allied MCM contributions (UK, Japan, France) would be essential but depend on political willingness to participate.
unit: LCS-based MCM mission packages deployed to 5th Fleet
political_cost: Mine countermeasures are invisible to the public but operationally critical; the gap between capability and requirement is a known DoD vulnerability that has received insufficient funding and attention.
source: USNI Fleet Trackers (October 2025, December 2025); Project knowledge (Iranian mine inventory, Strait mining data)

name: Domestic Public Support Context
description: As of early 2026 (pre-conflict), public support for military strikes against Iran stood at approximately 21% (University of Maryland poll, February 5-9, 2026), the lowest pre-conflict support for any major US military operation in modern polling history. For comparison, Iraq War support was 76% at inception, and Vietnam held majority support until 1967. The partisan gap is historically extreme: 90% of self-identified MAGA Republicans support strikes versus 6-7% of Democrats — an 83-point partisan divide. Independents oppose at 61%. Approximately 55% of Americans say Iran did not pose an imminent military threat, and roughly 65% say the President has not clearly explained war goals. Notably, significant opposition exists within the MAGA base itself: Tucker Carlson, Marjorie Taylor Greene ('MAGA was supposed to be America first, not Israel first'), Joe Rogan, Matt Walsh, and Joe Kent (who resigned from the administration) have publicly opposed the conflict. Pre-war structural models projected Republicans losing approximately 28 House seats in the 2026 midterms — enough to lose the chamber. This is the defining domestic constraint on sustained military operations.
unit: percent pre-war support for Iran strikes (University of Maryland, Feb 2026)
political_cost: The lowest initial public support for any comparable US military operation creates a political time limit on the conflict; 2026 midterms (November 3) represent the decisive political checkpoint, with 29 GOP-held House seats already in jeopardy.
source: University of Maryland poll (Feb 5-9, 2026); Project knowledge (full polling table, partisan breakdown, historical comparisons, midterm projections)
